## Implementation and Training Pipeline

### Configuration-Driven Design

Each experiment is fully specified by an external YAML scenario file covering
data paths, feature subsets, reward type, environment settings, and
optimization hyperparameters. No source-code changes are required to switch
between experimental variants. A pre-training validation stage checks path
availability, feature definitions, split feasibility, and column compatibility
before any computation begins, preventing wasted runs from misconfigured
experiments. A condensed example configuration is provided in Appendix C.

### Feature Pipeline

Feature engineering is registry-driven: feature names in the scenario file
resolve to computation functions, allowing scenario-level composition without
editing training code. This design is central to systematic feature subset
comparisons. Normalization parameters are estimated on the training split only
and applied without re-fitting to the validation and test data, preserving
causal integrity across the chronological split.

### Training Loop

The processed training split is used to construct the trading environment with
a continuous action space $a_t \in [-1, 1]$ representing target portfolio
exposure. Training follows the TD3 procedure described in Chapter 4: experience
is collected into a replay buffer, twin critics are updated from sampled
mini-batches, and the actor is updated every $d$ critic steps via the
deterministic policy gradient. Target networks track the main networks through
soft updates with coefficient $\tau$. Exploration noise is applied during
training and removed at evaluation time.

### Evaluation

Final performance is reported on the held-out test split using a separate
evaluation environment. Two output streams are kept strictly separate: the
training reward (an optimization diagnostic) and the realized portfolio return
series from which financial metrics are computed. Conflating these — for
example treating cumulative DSR as a Sharpe ratio estimate — would invalidate
the empirical comparison. Metrics derived from the test rollout are described
in Chapter 7.
